coredata.dc:title: Avocado seeds derived carbon dots for highly sensitive Cu (II)/Cr (VI) detection and copper (II) removal via flocculation
coredata.dc:creator: Mejía Ávila, J.
Rangel Ayala, M.
Kumar, Y.
Pérez-Tijerina, E
Robles, Mitchel A.R.
Agarwal, V.
coredata.dc:description: Sustainable, scalable and green carbon dots (CDs) were derived from avocado seeds using carbonization at different temperatures (250, 400, and 600 °C) and were applied as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of heavy metal ions. The CDs were found to be highly selective towards Cr6+ and Cu2+ ions and acted as fluorescent turn-off sensors. An increase in the carbonization temperature not only decreased the dimensions of the CDs from 4.6 to 3.2 nm, but also enhanced the crystallinity and sensitivity towards Cu2+/Cr6+. Although the CDs act as accurate optical sensors at low concentration range for Cu2+ ions, at higher concentration flocculation (visible to the naked eye) takes place and hence their applicability for possible water treatment is elucidated. The high sensitivity of CDs synthesized at 600 °C is revealed through a low limit of detection (LOD), i.e., 0.04 nM and 0.09 nM for Cr6+ and Cu2+ ions respectively. The efficacy of the proposed particles has been successfully demonstrated in the possible real-life applications via sensitivity tests in river/tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Avocado seeds derived carbon dots for highly sensitive Cu (II)/Cr (VI) detection and copper (II) removal via flocculation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physics and Mathematics (FCFM-UANL), Autonomous University of Nuevo Leon, Cd. Universitaria, San Nicolás de Los Garza, N.L. 66451, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sustainable, scalable and green carbon dots (CDs) were derived from avocado seeds using carbonization at different temperatures (250, 400, and 600 °C) and were applied as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of heavy metal ions. The CDs were found to be highly selective towards Cr6+ and Cu2+ ions and acted as fluorescent turn-off sensors. An increase in the carbonization temperature not only decreased the dimensions of the CDs from 4.6 to 3.2 nm, but also enhanced the crystallinity and sensitivity towards Cu2+/Cr6+. Although the CDs act as accurate optical sensors at low concentration range for Cu2+ ions, at higher concentration flocculation (visible to the naked eye) takes place and hence their applicability for possible water treatment is elucidated. The high sensitivity of CDs synthesized at 600 °C is revealed through a low limit of detection (LOD), i.e., 0.04 nM and 0.09 nM for Cr6+ and Cu2+ ions respectively. The efficacy of the proposed particles has been successfully demonstrated in the possible real-life applications via sensitivity tests in river/tap water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: In the field of materials science, carbon dots (CDs) are among the nanomaterials that exhibit numerous highly attractive properties, such as fluorescence, water-solubility, excellent thermo/photo-stability, and non– or low toxicity, as well as large-scale production viability [1,2]. The quasi-spherical nanosized (<10 nM) CDs [3] with graphitic, crystalline, or amorphous sp2 hybridized carbon core and an oxidized carbon surface have been successfully synthesized by “top-down” as well as “bottom-up” approach [4]. Specifically, top-down techniques consist of fragmentation of carbon matter into carbon nanoparticles by means of strategies such as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemistry, and wet oxidation, etc. On the other hand, the bottom-up approach, means conversion of small molecules with carbon precursors (such as citric acid and urea [5], glucose [6]) to CDs of required size, using hydrothermal, ultrasonic, thermal decomposition, pyrolysis, carbonization, microwave synthesis, and solvothermal method [7]. Among the bottom-up approaches, pyrolysis is widely used, where the organic material from the carbon source is converted into CDs by heating, dehydration, degradation, and carbonization under high temperatures in either vacuum or inert atmospheres [8]. Such CDs are categorized under sustainable nanotechnology due to the popular usage of innumerable plants, fruits, or even biowaste as carbon precursors [9,10] and have been applied in the fields of bioimaging, cancer therapy, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices (solar cells/light-emitting devices), catalysis, supercapacitors, agriculture, and optical sensors for pollutants and heavy metals [11-13].

On the other hand, pollution by heavy metals is a great concern due to enormously increasing negative implications to the environment and human health. Hexavalent chromium ions Cr6+ are broadly used in the textile industry, leather tanning, wood preservation, among others [14]. Being carcinogenic and mutagenic, Cr6+ is extremely hazardous for human health [15]. The US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) water regulation for Cr6+ in drinking water is 0.1 mg/l [16]. Although copper ions (Cu2+) are abundant and indispensable in living cells, due to its extreme toxicity after a certain permissible limit, it is also recognized as an important pollutant by the EPA [17]. According to the EPA, the allowable level of Cu2+ in drinking water is 30 µM [18].

Most of the current techniques to detect heavy metals imply either complex procedures or specialized and expensive equipment [19]. Recently, fluorometric approaches based on toxic [20,21] and non-toxic [22-25] chemical approaches have been investigated and considered as easy, fast, cost-effective, and reliable sensing techniques. CDs have been widely applied for the detection of heavy metal ions due to their ease in binding to the carboxyl and amine groups present on the surface of CDs, by coordination type bonding/electrostatic interaction or by free radical reaction [26]. Although detection ofCr (VI) from CDs derived from different other green precursors, such as lemon peel [27], tulsi leaves [28], pineapple juice [29], natural kelp [30], sophora flavescens [31],weissella sp.K1 [32], alkaloid-soluble poria cocos [33], and panax notoginseng [34] has been reported, the formed CDs demonstrate high quantifiable limits (i.e., low sensitivity) for possible practical viability. Similarly, several green sources such as saga waste [35], peanut shells [36], lemon juice [37], lignocellulosic waste [38], bamboo leaves [39],eleusine coracana [40], andspirulinaalgae [41] have been used for the detection of Cu 2+ ions, nevertheless, its flocculation using CDs has been scarcely reported [42].

In this work, CDs synthesized using avocado seeds (green waste) as carbon source have been demonstrated as fluorometric detectors/sensors for Cr6+ and Cu2+. Apart from low quantifiable/detection limit for Cr6+ and Cu2+, in a certain concentration range, the proposed CDs have been shown to cause flocculation/sedimentation in presence of Cu2+ ions (via complex formation) which makes them a highly attractive and versatile green option towards the water purification/treatment from the Cu2+ contamination.

Avocado seeds were collected from a local organically grown tree. All the salts, such as AgNO3 (99%), FeSO4 (99%), HgCl2 (99.5%), NiSO4 (98%), CuCl2 (97%) or K2Cr2O7 (99%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, NiSO4, Cd(NO3)2, ZnCl, and Mg(NO3) were obtained from Fermont and Rhodamine 6G from Hycel company. The deionized water was employed in all general experiments.

The absorbance was obtained using Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectra were measured through the Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer. The FTIR spectra were recorded using Varian 660-IR spectrophotometer, XRD measurements were acquired with PANanalytical X́pert3, XPS experiments were performed in a Thermo scientific Escalab 250Xi equipment, Z-potential was measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano series equipment and TEM images were obtained through TEM-JEOL JEM-2010 [43-47].

To investigate the selectivity of CDs towards different metal ions, 10 µL of CDs (3.8 mg/mL) were added to 490 µL of 2 mM of AgNO3, HgCl2, CuCl2, K2Cr2O7, NiSO4, Cd(NO3)2, ZnCl2, or Mg(NO3)2. To determine the relative sensitivity of CDs towards Cr6+ and Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at different concentrations of metallic solution in the CDs. For demonstrating the dependence of sensitivity on the relative concentration of CDs, a different volumetric ratio of 3:2 (stock soln of CDs: metal ion soln) was also tested for its viability as optical sensor at higher concentrations of Cr+6/Cu+2 (Results included in supplementary information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cr6+/Cu2+ sensitivity of CDs in real tap/river water
ce:section.ce:para: The practical viability of the synthesized nanoprobes for Cu2+ and Cr6+ ion detection/sensing has been demonstrated by testing them in the real water. Apart from the tap water taken from our laboratory in Cuernavaca, Morelos, México, water from the river (Apatlaco) located in Jojutla, Morelos, Mexico (coordinated positions given in the supplementary information) was used to demonstrate the efficacy of the nanoprobes. The collected river water was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 20 min to remove the suspended particles before measuring the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs in river water in the presence of Cu2+ and Cr6+ solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and microstructure of the CDs, analyzed through HRTEM (Fig. 1 ) images, revealed quasi-spherical and well dispersed CDs with an average size decreasing from 4.6 nm to 3.2 nm, with an increase in carbonization temperature from 250 °C to 600 °C respectively. In particular, the particle sizes varied from 2.8 −7.1 nm, 2.2 nm to 6.0 nm and 1.9 nm to 4.5 nm for the particles carbonized at 250 °C (Fig. 1(a)), 400 °C (Fig. 1(b)) and 600 °C (Fig. 1(c)) respectively. Similar to the formation mechanism proposed by Tao et al. in 2019, during the hydrothermal synthesis, the rise in the temperature helps the generation of intermolecular collisions causing dehydration of functional groups followed by an intense thermal motion promoting the reaction pathways for the formation of larger polymer chains. Hence with the increase in temperature, more intense thermal motion and newly initiated reaction pathways lead to the formation of cross-linked chains and clusters (microcrystalline) which form the core of CDs [1]. Tan et al., synthetized CDs from sago waste and found that carbonization temperatures below 400 °C could cause incomplete carbonization, and over 400 °C, they suggest a decomposition of the organic structure with the formation of ashes [35]. In the present case, an increase in the carbonization temperature until 600 °C results in a highly cross-linked or semi-graphitic compact structure of the core (decreasing the average dimension of CDs as the synthesis temperature increase) (Schematic S1b).

Additionally, in conformity with the previously reported huge decrease in the PL intensity with an increase in carbonization degree [1], in the present study, CD-250 showed the higher PL intensity (Fig. S1) with respect to the PL intensity from CD-400 and CD-600 (Fig. S2).

The XRD patterns of the CDs (Fig. 1(e)) reveal a principal diffraction peak centered around 27°, which is close to the characteristic peak of the graphitic (002) crystal plane at 26.1°[30]. Apart from the broader peak corresponding to CDs at 250 °C, an increase in the peak intensity with an increase in the carbonization temperature demonstrates an enhancement of the crystallinity of CDs at higher temperatures. Also, at all three temperatures the presence of a peak at 31° could possibly be associated with the presence of nitrogen in biomass generated CDs.

The XPS survey spectrum was performed to further validate the elemental composition and functional groups of the prepared CDs. The spectrum shows the existence of two pronounced peaks at C1s, O1s, and a very weak signal of N1s in the inset of Fig. 2 (a) for CD-600. Elemental analysis in atomic % are shown in Fig. S3. The deconvolution of the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2(b)) shows three well-resolved peaks located at 284.79, 288.2, and 287.7 eV, ascribed to the following different functional groups C = O, C–C [51], C = O/C = N [52] or C–C/C–H [53] and, C = O [54]. Similarly, for O1s spectrum (Fig. 2(c)), deconvolution leads to the four conspicuous peaks at 531.6 eV (C = O)/C-O [40,55], 532.7 eV (C-O) [56], 530.6 eV (could be associated with physically adsorbed oxygen on the surface of CDs) and, 530 eV (C-O) [30,57] associated to the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. Apart from this, a very weak nitrogen signal (Inset of Fig. 2(a)), observed at 400.1 eV, could be attributed to C-N [40,57,58]. Additionally, the presence of C = N at 288.2 eV also indicates the incorporation of N in the CDs (Fig. 2(b)). Moreover, as the synthesis temperature increases from 250 to 600 °C, the elemental analysis of C1s, O1s, and N1s reveals decreasing content of C1s (and nitrogen content) with a simultaneous increase in the oxygen content (Fig. S3). The existence of the oxygen containing functional groups has been attributed to the possible oxidation of free radicals present on the surface during open air carbonization and hence lead to an increase in the oxygen content. Furthermore, the raw material was studied by TGA (SI, Fig. S4) for better understanding of the carbonization process. The TGA results indicate the degradation of some biopolymers with higher molecular weight near to 600 °C and volatilization of heavy tars at temperatures > 600 0C [59].

To understand the presence of different functional groups, FT-IR analysis was carried out immediately after the open air carbonization process (Fig. 2(d)). The more notable absorption peaks of CDs are located around 3300 cm−1, which is a wideband corresponding to the stretching of O–H bond and overlapped with the vibration band of N–H (3400–3500 cm−1) (mainly for CD-250 and CD-400). In case of CD-600, the wideband corresponding to 3310 cm−1 is reduced and the OH signal is detected at 3678 cm−1 [60]. The double band at 2900–2970 cm−1 is congruent to the C–H stretching band [57,61] and 1560 cm−1 is the medium bending band vibration of N–H from NH2 scissoring [62]. The band at 1395 cm−1 is related to the in-plane C-O–H bending band [57], 1240 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of C-N [34], 1065 cm−1 denotes a stretching band of C-O [48,52,63] and close to 878 cm−1 a bending vibration corresponding to C–H [48,52] can be observed.

The presence of these oxygen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl contribute towards the extremely good water solubility of CDs. In addition, the active functional groups provide reaction sites that could bind with other molecules specifically [64].

The as-prepared CDs, dispersed in the water, show bluish fluorescence emission under UV exposure (365 nm). Fig. 2(e) and (f) show the absorption spectra for the CD-400 and CD-600. The absorption graph corresponding to the CD-250 is presented in Fig. S1(a). From the absorption spectra a wide band can be observed, without any apparent characteristic peak. A noticeable peak centered at 220 nm could be due to π- π* transition of C = C from the core [48]. The absence of absorption hump near 340 nm is attributed to the presence of a relatively broad size distribution of CDs [61]. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties (which increase with an increase in the synthesis temperature) of the CDs prepared at 250, 400 and 600 °C along with their analysis are discussed in the supplementary information (Fig. S1 (b,c) and Fig. S2). At high carbonization temperature (>300 °C), the origin of PL is attributed to the carbogenic core and the optical signal is considerably diminished due to the decrease in surface cross -linked polymer chains or remanent precursors. Although the carbon core is highly photostable, the PL centers are weaker than those from CDs surface defects (dominant in CDs synthetized at lower temperatures) [65,66].

The relative QY calculated with respect to Rhodamine 6G was 9.2 % for CDs obtained at 250 °C, and ∼ 2–3%, for CDs 400, and 600 °C respectively (details given in SI, Fig. S5). In addition, LOD decreases with an increase in the synthesis temperature (Fig. S10). Apart from the basic optical studies, their stability was tested in terms of their PL response at different pHs (2–14) (Fig. S6), salinity (0.5 to 2 M) (Fig. S7) and temperature (Figs. S8 and S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs based fluorometric Cr6+ and Cu2+ sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity studies: Depending upon the synthesis temperature, partial or almost complete PL signal quenching is observed in the presence of Cu2+ (PL quenching less than ∼ 26 % for CD-250, ∼87% for CD-400 and ∼ 95% for CD-600) and Cr6+ PL quenching <94 % for CD-250, 90% for CDs-400 and 96% for CD-600) (Fig. 3 (a), (c) and (e)). This indicates a strong affinity (>50 % PL quenching) between CDs and Cu2+ /Cr6+ ions, in particular valid for CDs synthesized at higher temperatures (400 and 600 °C). In contrast, other metal ions such as Ni2+, Hg2+, Ag2+, and Fe2+ showed a less notorious influence on the PL emissions (PL quenching less than ∼ 10 % for CD-250, ∼60% for CD-400 and ∼ 80% for CD-600) and this could be due to the low energy or charge transfer between metal ions and CDs [67]. In addition, CD-250 nanoprobes are only selective to Cr+6, whereas CD-400 and CD-600 were selective to Cr6+ as well as Cu2+ (Fig. 3(b), (d) and (f)).

As the CDs were found to be selective for Cu2+ and Cr6+, the sensitivity of the CDs (volumetric proportion of 1:49 i.e. 10µL of 3.8 mg/ml in the total volume of 490 µL) was evaluated at different concentrations of Cr6+ and Cu2+. It is observed that the PL intensity decreased with an increase in the concentration of Cr6+ (Fig. 4 (a) and 5(a)) and Cu2+ (Fig. 4(d) and 5(d)) along with a consistent shift in the emission peaks (results for CD-250 included in Figs. S11-S12). The observed shift in the PL peak wavelength opens the possibility of PL peak shift based dual confirmation of the unknown concentrations of Cr6+ or Cu2+. The sensitivity results have been further analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation (ref to Eq. S1 in SI) along with the estimation of LOD and LOQ (more details given in SI).

CDs fabricated at 400 °C exhibited detection range and linear fit from 0.01 to 0.05
μ M
with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9875 and the LOD is determined to be 5.5
nM
for Cr6+ ions (Fig. 4(b)). In the case of Cu2+, the very low detection range is from 0.01 to 0.05
μ M
and the R2 of 0.9727 along with LOD of 7.5
nM
(Fig. 4(e)). For the CD-600 nanoprobes, linear detection range of 0.5 to 0.9
nM ,
R2 of 0.9954 and LOD of 0.07
nM
(Fig. 5 (b)) is observed for Cr6+. Similarly, Cu2+ ions exhibited a linear relationship from 6–9 μ
M
, R2 of 0.9670 and LOD of 2
nM
for CDs at 600 °C (Fig. 5(e)). The CD-400 and CD-600 demonstrated another linear optical response in the range 50–300 µM, (3.8 mg/ml) (Fig. 4(c)) and 30–200 µM (Fig. 5(c)), respectively. In case of Cu2+, CD-600 showed relatively large linear range i.e., from 1.5 to 4 µM (Fig. 5(f)). For the CD-250, sensitivity details are presented in Fig. S11-12. The Stern-Volmer equations and parameters related to sensitivity analysis are summarized in Table S1. In addition, the sensitivity and LODs of the CDs in Cu2+ and Cr6+ measured for higher concentrations of CDs (3:2 volumetric proportion for CDs: metal ion solution) is given in Figs S13-S14 for CD-250, and Figs. S15 and S16 for CD-400 and CD-600, along with the summarized results in Table S2. The corresponding photographic images of CDs, dispersed in the different concentrations of Cr6+ (ranging from 10 to 10,000 µM), irradiated with UV (365 nm) and natural light, are shown in Fig. S17. Although the selectivity and sensitivity of different raw materials towards certain specific metal ion has been a challenge for the scientific community [50,57], the high sensitivity of the proposed nanoprobes is attributed to the complex inherent characteristics of the avocado seeds [68,69], which makes it highly medicinal [70,71] (although scarcely used). In general, avocado seed is high in carbohydrates (49 g/100 g), lipids (17.9 g/100 g), proteins (15.5 g/100 g), and moisture (15 g/100 g). In the lower range, it also possesses total oxalate (15 mg/100 g), tannin (7 mg/100 g), and phytic acid (3.2 mg/100 g). Additionally, phytosterols, triterpenes, fatty acids, and two glucosides of abscisic acid are present in the avocado seeds structure [72]. The properties of the proposed nanoprobes have been attributed to the combination of compounds (SI,S4), which after carbonization resulted in enough functionalized carbon chains (of high molecular weight) to propitiate the coordination with copper ions.

Although no flocculation was observed using CD-250 (Fig. 6 a), strong aggregation of the formed CDs + Cu2+ metallic complexes can be observed through naked eye for the CD-400 (Fig. 6b) and CD-600 before and after centrifugation (Fig. 6c). The CDs control solution (without the addition of Cu+2) revealed the PL emission which quenches completely after the flocculation. The increasing concentration of Cu+2 exhibits an increase in the amount of flocs (Fig. 6; insets in (b) (c)). The EDS analysis of the sedimented part (refer to Fig. S18) revealed a high content of copper ​​(36 ± 3.2% mass), carbon ​​(32.2 ± 4.3% mass), and oxygen ​​(21.1 ± 1.2% mass).

In order to verify the effect of CDs’ concentration on the flocculation process, a range of concentrations of CDs (0.07–1.9 mg/ml) were tested with different concentrations of Cu2+ (0.8 and 5 mM solution) (Fig. 7 ). Through naked eye ∼ 0.8 mM of Cu2+ is found to be the lowest concentration that can lead to flocculation, which initiates after ∼ 8 min of adding CDs to copper solution. The reference absorbance spectra, measured individually at different concentrations of CDs (Fig. 7a) and Cu2+ ions (inset Fig. 7a), reveal an increase in the optical signal with an increase in the corresponding concentration. The addition of Cu2+ ions to the solution containing CDs brings significant changes in the absorption spectra (Fig. 7b), i.e., emergence of a new band at 275 nm. The appearance of the new band could be attributed to the formation of CDs-Cu2+ ion complexes [73,74]. The concentration of formed complexes is found to increase with an increase in the concentration of CDs. The obtained absorbance tends towards a steady state as the concentration of CDs reaches to ∼ 1 mg/ml (inset Fig. 7b) for a concentration of 0.8 mM of Cu+2. Likewise, the addition of a larger concentration of Cu2+ ions (5 mM) results in a similar trend, albeit with relatively higher absorbance (inset Fig. 7b; also refer to Fig. S19a). The results with the same tendency were obtained with fluorescence measurements (Fig. 7c) and hence, ∼1 mg/ml could be recommended as the initial concentration of the proposed CDs for practical operation purposes. Flocculation experiments were also performed in tap water and comparable results were obtained (ref to Fig. S19b). Absorbance of CDs + Cu2+ in tap water (Fig. S19b), also shows the formation of the band corresponding to the metallic complex. Due to the flocculation the use of the nanoprobes as sensors is limited to low concentration range of Cu2+ only.

Although the practical application of CDs as metal ions sensors has demonstrated its effectiveness, the fluorescence origin and quenching mechanisms are still not completely clear. Most of the emission quenching mechanisms proposed for metal ions reported so far, are based on either aggregation-induced emission quenching (AIEQ), inner filter effect (IFE), or energy/charge transfer (CT) [75].

In the present study, the presence of Cr6+ induced the efficient PL quenching in as-prepared CD-400 and CD-600 nanoprobes. To understand the nature of the interaction between Cr6+ and CDs, Zeta potential (Z-potential) measurements were carried out. The Z-potential of the CDs-600 (2.3 mg/mL) and CDs-600 + Cr6+ (4 mM) solutions were found to be −57.8 and −40.06 ± 1.2 mV, respectively. The increase in the magnitude of the Z- potential (becoming less negative) after the addition of the Cr6+ ion, suggests the possible electrostatic interaction between CDs and Cr6+ (adsorption of Cr6+ onto the surface of CDs) [76]. Additionally, the Z-potential greater than + 30 mV or less than −30 mV provides information on the stability of the CDs i.e., CDs are highly dispersed [77] due to dominant repulsive forces that exist between the particles. In contrast, if the value of Z-potential lies between + 10 mV and −10 mV, they are believed neutral [77] and tend to agglomerate/coagulate.

The IFE [78-80] appears due to the absorption of the excitation signal or the emission from the CDs by absorbers in the detection system, i.e., when the absorption spectra of the absorbers overlap with the PL excitation or emission spectra of the fluorophores, the signal quenching takes place. Also, IFE has been considered an effective and efficient tool for converting the analytical absorption signals into fluorescence signals, which have been found to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity compared to other fluorescence quenching mechanisms. In the present case, Cr6+ solution shows broad absorption peaks centered at 260, 360, and 450 nm (Schematic 1). Since there is an overlap between the absorption spectrum of the quencher (Cr6+) as well as the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs, corresponding signal quenching due to the IFE is highly probable (Schematic 1, top) [14,81]. Also, IFE is the most common explanation for Cr6+ induced PL signal quenching reported in the literature [82,83].

In case of Cu+2, only report from Liu, et al.[42] used carrots and acrylamide based hydrothermally formed water-soluble CDs to show strong flocculation for Cu2+ ions, where PL quenching was attributed to multi-site coordination of metal ions with N /O atom of the functional groups. In the present work, the CDs were prepared from waste biomass as an only source (green synthesis method) without any additional passivation step. At higher synthesis temperature (CDs-600), some functional groups could possibly be detached from the CDś surface and lead to the increase in the compactness of the insoluble core, which might favor the metal ion's coordination while possibly avoiding steric hindrance [84] and resulting in the sedimentation.

Coagulation/flocculation in wastewater is the aggregation of solids achieved with the help of chemicals or other substances to alter the physical state of dissolved and suspended solids to facilitate the formation of larger aggregates and their removal via subsequent sedimentation process [85]. There is a high affinity between heavy metals and flocculants, such as charge neutralization, polymer binding, and electrostatic interactions [86]. Charge neutralization refers to the sorption of two particles with oppositely charged ions [87]. The effect of neutralizing the charge is revealed through the reduction of the electrokinetic effect i.e., Z-potential. Decrease in Z-potential promotes the creation of Van der Waals’ attractive forces, facilitating aggregation until larger particles and sedimentation of formed flocks [88]. Herein, the Z-potential was reduced from −63.2 mV to −7 mV for CDs-600 (2.3 mg/mL CDs + 4 mM Cu+2), giving evidence of flocculation via charge neutralization. As compared to the drastic increase in the Z-potential of CDs-600 (1.9 mg/mL, Z-potential = -57.8 mV) with an increase in Cu2+ concentrations (Table S3), the difference in the Z-potential was relatively stable after a 2.5 mM of Cu2+. Also, when the Zeta potential becomes more positive, the solution became acidic (pH changed from 8 to 6), which implies the existence of positively charged surroundings of the CDs (ionization of the basic groups OH– can result in the positively charged CDs) [89]. Additionally, it is known that Cu2+, in comparison with other metal ions, possess a higher binding attraction and faster chelating kinetics towards dissolved organic carbon and particulate organic carbon, iron oxides (including hydrous ferric oxide), goethite and manganese oxides (including hydrous manganese oxide) due to the presence of typical N, O-chelate ligands on the surface of the CDs [40,90,91]. The obtained results support the possible aggregation-induced emission quenching mechanism attributed to the cross-linking of Cu2+ ions onto N and O ligands (from amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups) due to their strong chelation and electrostatic interactions. On the similar grounds, fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Cu2+ can be attributed to their aggregation. This effect is in conformity with the previous reports where the verification was done through DLS using classical-quantum dots (QDs) based chemosensors [92] and TEM using CDs from acacia concinna seeds [93]. In the present work, the aggregation could be observed simply by naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of selectivity/sensitivity in tap/river water sample

ce:section.ce:para: In this study, CDs from avocado seeds were satisfactorily obtained by a simple one-step carbonization technique. The extensive characterization was performed using high resolution transmission electron microscope, XPS, XRD and FTIR spectroscopy. The synthesis temperature tunable optical properties, morphology, and crystallinity not only disseminate through particle size reduction (from 4.6 to 3.2 nm) but also makes them more selective towards Cr6+ and Cu2+. Using absorbance spectroscopy and Z-potential analysis, the Cr6+ sensing mechanism of the proposed nanoprobes has been explained through inner filter effect. The flocculation/sedimentation caused by the proposed nanoprobes in the presence of Cu2+, gave an additional advantage of their simultaneous usage as flocculating agents (approx. Cu2+ concentration more than or equal to 800 µM) as well as optical sensors (Cu2+ concentration less than 800 µM in river water). The concentration of the CDs with respect to the metal ions not only facilitated the low concentration range of the metal ions which can be detected, but also revealed the CD concentration dependent sensitivity and tunable linear response of the proposed nanoprobes. The high sensitivity of CDs in spiked river water samples, revealed through an extremely low limit of detection (i.e., 0.04 and 0.09 nM for Cr6+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively), opens their potential applicability as sustainable, biocompatible and simple-to-use optical sensors. Their exclusive flocculating capacity can be further explored in wastewater treatment.
